6: David's heart striking him for cutting the robe-hem of Saul encodes the anti-grasping theology that runs from Genesis 3:6 to Philippians 2:6. What David takes in the dark cave is not yet his to take — the robe-hem, as a symbol of royal authority in ANE legal practice, is not yet David's even though the kingdom has been promised to him. His conscience reacts immediately: he has symbolically appropriated what YHWH has promised but not yet given. This is the precise anti-type of the Adamic and Satanic temptations: to grasp at what God has not yet granted, to accelerate the divine timetable by force. Christ's temptation in the wilderness (Matthew 4:8-10: the devil offers all the kingdoms of the world if Jesus will worship him) is the cosmic version of the Adullam cave: the shortcut to the kingdom without the cross. Jesus refuses on the same ground David refuses: not because the kingdom is not his but because the Father's means and the Father's time are not negotiable. Paul's description of the Incarnation (Philippians 2:6: 'he did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped') is the definitive theological statement of what David dimly enacts in the cave: the one who has the right to all refuses to take it before the appointed time.
15: David's appeal to YHWH as judge — YHWH yihyeh lᵉdayyān bênî ûbênêḵā, 'YHWH will be judge between me and you' — and his forensic self-deprecation ('after a dead dog, after a single flea') constitute the OT's most explicit narrative enactment of the principle that 1 Peter 2:23 identifies as Christ's defining response to unjust suffering: 'when he suffered, he did not threaten; instead, he committed himself to him who judges justly.' Peter is reading David — and through David, reading Christ. The righteous sufferer who does not take vengeance but appeals to the divine judge is the prophetic portrait of Christ's passion: the one who could have called twelve legions of angels (Matthew 26:53) choosing instead to commit his cause to the Father. The divine court to which David appeals is the same court to which Christ entrusts his cause; the vindication that David trusts YHWH to deliver is the resurrection that the Father grants on Easter morning — the ultimate verdict of the divine judge in favor of the righteous sufferer.
1: Saul returns from the Philistine campaign and is told David is in the wilderness of En-gedi. The irony of the information exchange is subtle: Saul's spies report David's location immediately after YHWH's providential rescue at Sela-hammahlekot. The renewed pursuit — after divine intervention deflected it — demonstrates that the persecutor's intent is not reformed by failure. The repetition of the pursuit after the temporary grace recurs in John's Gospel: Jesus escapes repeated arrest attempts, each time returning to the same vulnerable proximity to his enemies.
2: Saul takes 3,000 chosen men from all Israel to seek David and his men on the rocks of the wild goats. The 3,000 elite warriors against David's 600 maintains the overwhelming force ratio of the entire fugitive narrative. The wild goat rocks (ṣûrê hayyᵉʿēlîm) are the terrain of the most agile animals — the fugitive king moves where armies cannot easily follow. The anointed in his weakness occupies the crags that power cannot easily reach.
3: Saul comes to the sheep pens by the road where there was a cave (mᵉʿārāh), and he enters to relieve himself (lᵉhāsēḵ raglāyw, lit. to cover his feet). David and his men are sitting in the innermost parts of the cave. The accidental entry of the enemy into the anointed's hiding place — for the most mundane biological reason — creates the scene of maximum temptation. Providence places the persecutor in the grasp of the persecuted. The king of Israel, unarmed and vulnerable, enters the cave of the one he hunts.
4: David's men urge him: hinnēh hayyôm ʾăšer ʾāmar YHWH ʾēlêḵā hinnēh ʾānōḵî nōtēn ʾet ʾōyibᵉḵā bᵉyādeḵā — behold the day of which YHWH said to you, “I will give your enemy into your hand.” Whether YHWH actually said this or whether the men are improvising a prophetic word to justify killing Saul is ambiguous. The temptation is framed in theological language — using YHWH's name to justify the seizure of what has not yet been properly given. The same temptation faces Jesus: the devil quotes Scripture to urge premature grasping of divine inheritance (Matthew 4:6-7).
5: David cuts off the hem of Saul's robe (wayyāqom dāwid wayyikrot ʾet kᵉnap hamᵉʿîl ʾăšer lᵉŠāʾûl ballāṭ) — secretly, while Saul remains unaware. The kānāp (hem, wing) of the robe is symbolically significant: the robe represents royal authority; its hem could be used for sealing documents. When David later holds up the hem in v11, it proves proximity without bloodshed. But the cutting itself — even this small act — strikes David's conscience.
7: David restrains his men: ḥālîlāh lî mēYHWH ʾim ʾeʿĕśeh ʾet haddāḇār hazzeh laʾdōnî limšîaḥ YHWH lišlōaḥ yādî bô kî mᵉšîaḥ YHWH hûʾ — far be it from me before YHWH to do this thing to my lord, YHWH's anointed, to stretch out my hand against him, for he is YHWH's anointed. The repetition of mᵉšîaḥ YHWH — the LORD's anointed — twice in the restraint formula makes this the theological center of the En-gedi cave scene. David will not touch the LORD's anointed even though the anointed is his murderous persecutor. The ethic of non-retaliation against those whom YHWH has ordained — even when they pursue you unjustly — is the precise ethic of 1 Peter 2:23: when he was reviled, he did not revile in return.
8: David rebukes his men and does not let them attack Saul: wayyišsaʿ dāwid ʾet ʾănāšāyw badᵉḇārîm — David persuaded his men with words. The community of the anointed is held back from premature violence by the word of the anointed. The restraint extends from David to his followers — the non-retaliation ethic is not merely personal but communal. Jesus explicitly commands this for his disciples: Put your sword back in its place (Matthew 26:52), restraining Peter's retaliatory strike at the arrest.
9: Saul exits the cave and David follows: wayyāqom dāwid wayyēṣēʾ min hamᵉʿārāh wayyiqrāʾ ʾaḥărê Šāʾûl lēʾmōr ʾădōnî hammélek — my lord the king! David calls to the departing Saul. The scene of the anointed calling after his persecutor — emerging from the cave, revealing his presence, demonstrating vulnerability — is an act of radical trust. He could have remained hidden; instead he shows himself. The willingness of the innocent to make themselves known to those who seek to destroy them is the posture of the cross: Jesus is not hidden when they come to arrest him in Gethsemane but says ʾEgō eimi — I am he (John 18:5).
10: David bows to the ground (wayyiqod ʾerets wayyišteḥû). He addresses Saul: lāmmāh tišmaʿ ʾet diḇrê ʾādām lēʾmōr hinnēh dāwid mᵉḇaqqēš rāʿāteḵā — why do you listen to the words of men saying, “David seeks your harm”? The address to the pursuer with prostration and direct correction — naming the slander and refusing to match it — demonstrates the non-retaliatory ethic in action. The anointed does not defend himself with equal violence but with truth, humility, and appeal to the evidence.
11: David shows the hem of Saul's robe: rᵉʾēh kî ʾēt kᵉnap mᵉʿîlᵉḵā bᵉyādî — look, the hem of your robe is in my hand. The physical evidence of the opportunity not taken — the proof that he could have killed but did not — is the testimony that verbal claims cannot equal. kî bᵉkārᵉtî ʾet kᵉnap mᵉʿîlᵉḵā wᵉlōʾ hăragutiḵā dāʿ ûrᵉʾēh kî ʾên bᵉyādî rāʿāh ûpešaʿ wᵉlōʾ ḥāṭāʾtî lāḵ — in cutting the hem of your robe and not killing you, know and see that there is no evil or treason in my hand, and I have not sinned against you. The evidence of self-restraint is greater than any oath.
12: YHWH yišpoṭ bênî ûḇênêḵā ûnᵉqāmanî YHWH mimmennu ûyādî lōʾ tihyeh bāḵ — may YHWH judge between me and you; may YHWH avenge me against you, but my hand shall not be against you. The appeal to YHWH as judge rather than taking personal vengeance is the OT's clearest statement of the retribution-to-God principle. Romans 12:19 quotes Deuteronomy 32:35 in the same vein: vengeance is mine, I will repay. David models what Paul commands: bless the persecutor, do not avenge yourself, let YHWH be the judge.
13: kaʾăšer yōʾmar mᵉšal haqqadmōnî mērᵉšāʿîm yēṣēʾ rešaʿ ûyādî lōʾ tihyeh bāḵ — as the ancient proverb says, “Out of the wicked comes wickedness,” but my hand shall not be against you. The appeal to proverbial wisdom grounds the non-violence in folk wisdom as well as theology. The actions proceed from the character of the actor: from the wicked, wickedness; from the anointed who refuses wickedness, the evidence will speak itself.
14: David asks: ʾaḥar mî yāṣāʾ melek yiśrāʾēl ʾaḥar mî ʾattāh rōdēp — after whom has the king of Israel come out? After whom do you pursue? ʾaḥar keleb mēt ʾaḥar parʿōš ʾeḥāt — after a dead dog! After a single flea! The self-deprecation is rhetorical but also genuinely humble: David does not inflate his own threat. The disproportion of the king's military campaign against one fugitive is highlighted through the dead-dog and flea imagery. Jesus's silence before Pilate and Herod has the same structural quality: the greatest threat the authorities imagined was in fact the most humble figure they had ever encountered.
16: Saul's response: qôlᵉḵā bᵉnî dāwid wayyissā Šāʾûl qōlô wayyēḇĕ — is this your voice, my son David? And Saul wept. The persecutor, confronted with the evidence of the anointed's restraint and non-retaliation, weeps. The hard heart is momentarily softened by the sight of mercy. Saul's weeping is genuine but not lasting — it produces temporary remorse without lasting repentance, like Esau's weeping (Hebrews 12:17) or Judas's remorse (Matthew 27:3-5). The tears of the momentarily convicted are not the repentance of the renewed.
17: Saul confesses: ṣaddîq ʾattāh mimmennî kî ʾattāh gᵉmaltanî haṭṭôḇāh wᵉʾānōḵî gᵉmaltîḵā hārāʿāh — you are more righteous than I, for you have repaid me good and I have repaid you evil. The explicit acknowledgment of the anointed's righteousness and his own wickedness from the mouth of the persecutor is the En-gedi scene's theological climax. The evidence of the unretaliating anointed produces confession from the persecutor. The NT equivalent: the centurion at the cross — truly this was the Son of God (Matthew 27:54), the persecutors' representative confessing what the resurrection will vindicate.
18: wᵉʾattāh higgadtā ʾēt ʾăšer ʿāśîtā lî ṭōḇāh ʾēt ʾăšer sᵉgāranî YHWH bᵉyādeḵā wᵉlōʾ hᵉragātnî — you have declared today what good you have done to me, that YHWH gave me into your hand and you did not kill me. Saul explicitly names what David did: YHWH gave the persecutor into the anointed's hand, and the anointed let him go. The king acknowledges that his survival depends entirely on the mercy of the one he pursues.
19: wᵉkî yimṣā ʾîš ʾet ʾōyibô ûšilᵉḥô bᵉdereḵ ṭôḇāh wᵉYHWH yᵉšallimᵉḵā ṭôḇāh taḥat hayyôm hazzeh ʾăšer ʿāśîtā lî — for if a man finds his enemy and sends him away on the good road, may YHWH reward you with good for what you did to me today. Saul provides the theological interpretation of David's act: sending an enemy on the good way is the proper response to mercy. YHWH yᵉšallimᵉḵā — may YHWH repay you — is an implicit recognition that the ultimate repayment belongs to YHWH, not to Saul.
20: Saul's prophetic acknowledgment: wᵉʿattāh yādaʿtî kî mālōḵ timlōḵ ûqāmāh bᵉyādᵉḵā mamleket yiśrāʾēl — I know that you shall surely be king, and that the kingdom of Israel shall be established in your hand. The persecutor confesses the anointed's destiny from his own mouth. Pilate's inscription — Yeshua of Nazareth, King of the Jews (John 19:19) — is Saul's confession restaged: the authority that prosecutes the anointed inadvertently testifies to his kingship.
21: Saul asks David to swear: wᵉʿattāh hišābaʿ lî baYHWH ʾim tašmîd ʾet zarʿî ʾaḥărāy wᵉʾim tašmîd ʾet šᵉmî mibêt ʾāḇî — swear to me by YHWH that you will not cut off my descendants and that you will not destroy my name from my father's house. Saul's request — preserve my family even when you have the power to destroy them — is the practical application of his confession. He knows the pattern of new dynasties eliminating rival claimants. His fear is theologically informed: the messianic king will be defined by his mercy.
22: David swears to Saul, and Saul goes home while David and his men go up to the stronghold. The covenant of mercy between the persecutor and the persecuted is made on the basis of the anointed's demonstrated restraint. David's later preservation of Mephibosheth (2 Sam 9) fulfills this oath. Jesus's preservation of all given to him by the Father (John 17:12; 1 Samuel 18:9) is the ultimate fulfillment: those whom you gave me I have kept, and none of them is lost.
